items.myth: Real engineers don't use a debugger.
items.reality: Every senior Python engineer reaches for `breakpoint()` regularly. The myth is that print-debugging is somehow purer; in fact it's just slower. Use whichever tool finds the bug fastest.
items.example: A senior often `breakpoint()`s once, inspects three variables, and is done — where a junior runs the script 10 times with prints.

items.reality: `breakpoint()` is a built-in that defaults to calling `pdb.set_trace()`; it's just the modern, swappable entry point. Override via `PYTHONBREAKPOINT` to use ipdb or anything else.
items.example: `PYTHONBREAKPOINT=ipdb.set_trace python script.py` — same `breakpoint()` call, different debugger.

items.reality: pytest plays nicely with pdb. `pytest --pdb` drops into pdb on the first failure; `breakpoint()` in a test or fixture works. `pytest --trace` even starts every test in the debugger.
items.example: `pytest --pdb -x` — stop at first failure, drop into pdb at the line that failed.

items.reality: It's the easiest path to a hard bug. Run `python -m pdb -c continue script.py`; when the exception happens you're already at the right frame. No reproduction work.
items.example: Jupyter: `%pdb on` and any exception drops you into the offending frame automatically.

items.myth: pdb is too slow for big programs.
items.reality: pdb's overhead is meaningful inside the running tracer but doesn't matter outside the debug session. Use it on a focused failure; for performance issues use cProfile, not pdb.
items.example: Use cProfile to find the slow function; use pdb to inspect why.
